blocks.text: Chapter 5.
Evaluate Like an Investor: Criteria, Opportunity Cost, Margin of Safety
The ideas in 30 seconds

Options can only be evaluated against explicit criteria, and good criteria have three properties: they are clear enough to explain to a twelve-year-old, they promote the actual goal rather than a proxy, and they are decisive enough to single out exactly one best option.
The targeting principle demands you name the one criterion that matters most — when everything is a priority, nothing is, and trade-offs get made by accident instead of by design.

Feed your evaluation with HiFi (high-fidelity) information gathered firsthand and HiEx (high-expertise) information from people who earned their knowledge — and learn to distinguish real experts from imitators before you weight their advice.

Every option carries an opportunity cost — what the same time, money, and energy could have bought elsewhere — and the do-nothing option must be evaluated explicitly, because sometimes it beats every active alternative.
Build a margin of safety: choose options you could survive even if the worst case turned out twice as bad as you predict, because the future is less knowable than your forecasts pretend.

Time decisions deliberately: make cheap, reversible calls as soon as possible and consequential, irreversible ones as late as possible — and end deliberation the moment you stop learning, start losing opportunities, or already know the answer.
Good Criteria Are Clear, Promote the Goal, and Pick a Winner

Once you have defined the real problem and generated at least three options (Chapter 4), evaluation begins — and evaluation is only as good as the criteria you evaluate against. Most people skip this step, compare options on vague gut feel, and end up choosing whatever the emotion, ego, social, or inertia default prefers. Parrish gives criteria three non-negotiable properties.

First, clarity. Criteria must be simple, specific, and free of jargon — the test is whether you could explain them to a twelve-year-old. "We want the best candidate" or "the option with the most synergy" sounds rigorous but decides nothing, because fuzzy language lets every option qualify. Fuzzy criteria are usually a symptom of fuzzy thinking about the problem itself.

Second, goal promotion. Each criterion must actually advance the objective the decision exists to serve, not a convenient proxy. Hiring committees that quietly optimize for "candidate we'd enjoy having lunch with" instead of "candidate who can do the job" are using criteria that fail this test. Whenever a criterion drifts in, ask: if an option maximized this, would it solve the problem we defined?

Third, decisiveness. Applied to your options, the criteria must identify exactly one best choice. If two options tie, your criteria are not doing their job — a tie means you have not yet decided what matters.

Decisiveness is enforced by the targeting principle: know the one most important thing the decision must achieve, and rank it explicitly above everything else. A decision with five "equally important" priorities is rudderless; real options differ, so a genuinely ranked list will always break ties. Naming the single most-important criterion also defends the decision later — when stakeholders lobby for their pet factor, you can show that the trade-off was chosen deliberately, not stumbled into. Write the criteria down before you look at options, so the options can't quietly rewrite the standards they'll be judged by.
Gather HiFi Information: Get Close to the Source

Evaluation runs on information, and information degrades every time it passes through another person's summary, agenda, or interpretation. Parrish's first quality dimension is HiFi information — high-fidelity knowledge that is accurate, detailed, and as close to the source as possible.

Three practices raise fidelity. First, do a trial run or experiment before committing. Instead of debating whether a candidate, vendor, or career move will work, find a cheap way to test it — a paid project before a full-time hire, a pilot before a rollout. Firsthand results beat any amount of secondhand argument.

Second, go see for yourself. Reports, dashboards, and slide decks are abstractions built by people with their own incentives; visiting the factory floor, sitting in on the sales call, or reading the raw customer complaints gives you what the summary filtered out. The people closest to the problem usually hold the highest-fidelity information in the organization — and they are the people most decision-makers never talk to.

Third, keep your own views quiet while you gather. The moment you announce what you think, two distortions kick in: people start tailoring their input to please or oppose you, and your ego default starts defending the stated position instead of testing it. Ask specific, open questions — "What would have to be true for this to fail?" beats "Any thoughts?" — and let others finish before you reveal your leaning.

The common thread is humility about transmission: every intermediary between you and reality is a chance for noise, spin, and omission to creep in. High-stakes decisions deserve at least some information that no one else has pre-chewed for you. When you can't get to the source directly, the next best thing is borrowing someone who has spent a career there — which is where high-expertise information comes in.
Consult Experts, Not Imitators

The second quality dimension is HiEx information — knowledge from people with genuine, earned expertise. An hour with someone who has spent decades in the terrain of your problem compresses years of trial and error into a conversation, and it is almost always cheaper than the mistake it prevents. But expert input only helps if you consult well and if the person is actually an expert.

Consulting well means treating experts as instruments of your thinking, not replacements for it. Don't ask them to make the decision. Give context: the problem as you've defined it, what you've already tried, your constraints, and what you specifically want from them. Ask how they think about problems like yours — their reasoning generalizes; their bottom-line answer may not fit your situation. And remember the decision, and responsibility for it, stays with you.

The harder skill is telling experts from imitators, because imitators have mastered the vocabulary and confidence of expertise without its substance. Parrish's tells: imitators can't explain things in plain language — jargon is their camouflage, and requests to simplify expose them. They get frustrated or defensive when you ask fundamental questions, where real experts enjoy them. They talk about their wins but never their failures, while genuine experts carry scars and discuss them openly. They can't tell you what they've changed their mind about, because their knowledge was copied, not earned through updating against reality. And imitators tend to broadcast — dropping names and credentials — where experts let the quality of their reasoning carry the weight.

One more filter: expertise is domain-specific. A brilliant surgeon's opinion on your business strategy is a smart amateur's opinion. Weight advice by earned expertise in this problem's domain, not by general accomplishment or status.
Opportunity Cost and the Power of Doing Nothing

Every option's true price includes what you give up to take it. The opportunity cost principle says to evaluate options in terms of the alternative uses of the same money, time, and energy — the invisible costs that don't appear on any invoice. Most bad deals look good only because no one asked what else the resources could have done.

Parrish supplies three questions to surface hidden costs. "Compared with what?" forces an explicit baseline — an option is never good or bad in isolation, only relative to alternatives. "And then what?" extends the second-level thinking from the option-generation stage into evaluation: trace the consequences of consequences before pricing an option. "At the expense of what?" names the sacrifice directly — the promotion at the expense of family time, the acquisition at the expense of balance-sheet flexibility.

It also helps to convert costs into a common unit. Translate money into hours of your life, or a recurring commitment into total annual hours, and options that looked incomparable suddenly rank cleanly. A "free" board seat that consumes 100 hours a year has a very visible price once denominated in your scarcest resource.

Finally, always evaluate the do-nothing option as a real candidate, not a failure state. The status quo is a choice, and sometimes it outperforms every active alternative — acting for the sake of acting is the emotion and social defaults dressed up as decisiveness. Putting "change nothing" on the list does two jobs: it sets the bar every option must clear, and it exposes cases where all your options are just differently flavored losses. If nothing beats doing nothing, the disciplined move is to wait, keep gathering information, and let better options emerge.
Margin of Safety: Survive a Worst Case Twice as Bad

Forecasts fail, and the future is under no obligation to respect your projections. The margin of safety — a concept borrowed from engineering and value investing, where bridges are built to carry far more than their expected load — is the buffer between what you expect and what you could survive. Parrish's baseline test is blunt: could you absorb the worst case if it turned out twice as bad as you predict? If a job change assumes you'll find new work in three months, could you survive six? If a project's downside is a $50,000 loss, could you take $100,000 and stay in the game?

The doubling isn't pessimism; it's an honest correction for the fact that worst-case estimates are themselves optimistic — they're built from the same assumptions that produced the base case. A margin of safety converts "this should work" into "this works even when I'm wrong," which is the only kind of plan that survives contact with reality.

Size the margin to the stakes and the uncertainty. Routine, recoverable decisions need little buffer, and padding them wastes resources. Decisions that touch survival — solvency, health, reputation, irreplaceable relationships — need generous ones, because no expected upside compensates for ruin. The math is asymmetric: a margin of safety costs a slice of best-case upside, but it eliminates outcomes you cannot come back from. Keeping a cash runway, borrowing less than the bank approves, leaving schedule slack in the critical path — each trades a little efficiency for the ability to be wrong and continue.

The margin of safety also compounds with the book's positioning theme from Part 1: buffers keep bad luck from forcing your hand, so you're never compelled to make a desperate decision at the worst possible moment. The strong position isn't the one that wins the forecast; it's the one that survives the forecast being wrong.
Timing: ASAP, ALAP, and Knowing When to Stop Deliberating

Not every decision deserves the same clock. Parrish splits them on two axes — consequentiality and reversibility — and assigns opposite timing rules. Cheap, reversible, low-stakes decisions should be made as soon as possible (ASAP): extra deliberation buys nothing, delay clogs your attention, and any error is easily undone. Consequential, hard-to-reverse decisions should be made as late as possible (ALAP) — not because slowness is virtuous, but because time delivers information and preserves optionality, and you can't un-ring the bell. ALAP means the last responsible moment, not procrastination: you keep working the problem while the window stays open.
But how do you know the window is closing? Parrish gives three signals that deliberation is over and it's time to decide.

Stop: you've stopped gathering useful information. The inputs are repeating, the analysis is re-analysis, and more research is just anxiety wearing a productive costume. When new effort stops changing the picture, more effort won't either.

Lost opportunity: you're about to lose an option by not choosing. The moment delay starts foreclosing alternatives — the candidate takes another offer, the price window closes — the cost of waiting has flipped from negative to positive, and further deliberation is itself a decision with a price.

Know: you already know what to do. When the evaluation clearly points one way, waiting adds risk, not insight — the hesitation is usually the social or ego default wanting cover, not a genuine information gap. Certainty you refuse to act on decays into doubt.

Any one signal is sufficient. Together with the earlier tools — ranked criteria, HiFi and HiEx information, opportunity-cost accounting, and a margin of safety — they turn evaluation from an open-ended agonizing session into a bounded process with a defined exit. What remains is executing the choice and judging it fairly, the subject of the next chapter.
In practice
Before comparing options on your next significant decision, write down your criteria and test each one: could a twelve-year-old understand it, does it serve the real goal, and will it break ties?
Circle the single most important criterion and rank the rest — refuse to let two priorities share first place.

Get one piece of HiFi information firsthand this week: run a small experiment, visit the source, or interview the person closest to the problem — and keep your own opinion to yourself while you do it.
Before taking any expert's advice, apply the imitator screen: ask them to explain in plain language, ask what they've failed at, and ask what they've changed their mind about.
Add "do nothing" as an explicit option to every decision you're evaluating, and ask of the leading option: compared with what, and then what, at the expense of what?

Stress-test your current biggest commitment against a worst case twice as bad as forecast; if it wouldn't survive, add buffer now — and for any decision you're sitting on, check the three exit signals: no new information, an option about to expire, or an answer you already know.
